iterations.strategy: Root cause (Category A + C — plan restructuring + work elimination): data_loading consumed 97% of total time (2511ms) because mmap_prefetch_all eagerly read all 5 num columns (~828MB) from disk, while zone maps show only 1-3 of 395 blocks contain USD 2023 data (~0.17% combined selectivity = ~67K rows, ~4MB of column data needed). A secondary cost was building a 130MB unordered_set from 8.6M keys (~800ms), and 64 thread-local 12MB direct arrays generating a 106ms merge phase. Fix: load zone maps first (4.7KB), identify surviving block indices, mmap columns without prefetch, then madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) on only the surviving block byte ranges per column (~4MB total vs 828MB). Replace unordered_set with vector<uint64_t> + std::lower_bound — avoids 130MB hash table construction while adding only ~30ms for 67K binary searches. Switch to single-threaded scan (1-3 blocks is trivially fast) with compact unordered_map aggregation (~200KB, L2-resident). Expected: data_loading ~30-50ms, scan ~30ms, total ~60-80ms.

Root cause (Category B — algorithm change): main_scan consumed 76% of total time (54ms) because 64-thread parallel binary search performed 67K × 23 = 1.5M random accesses into the 69MB pre_adsh_tagver_set sorted array, which exceeds the 44MB LLC. Even with pre-faulting (5ms build_joins), the binary search bottom-levels miss L3 and hit DRAM at random offsets. Fix: replace random binary search with sort-merge anti-join. Collect all ~67K valid probe entries {uint64_t key, int32_t tagver_code, double value} (1.3MB total, L2-resident) in a single sequential pass; sort by key (~0.5ms, fully in-cache); then single-pass merge with the sorted anti array — a sequential 69MB read at memory bandwidth (~1-2ms with MADV_SEQUENTIAL and hardware prefetch). Also eliminate the build_joins pre-fault phase (5ms) since the sequential merge naturally triggers hardware prefetch on a hot page cache. Switch to single-threaded: 67K rows is trivial and thread overhead dominated at this scale. Expected: collect+sort+merge = ~3ms, total ~5-10ms.

Root cause (Category B — algorithm change): main_scan=54ms is dominated by 8.9M binary searches (actual qualifying row count, not 67K as guide estimated) into the 69MB sorted anti array that exceeds the 44MB LLC. Each probe reaches the DRAM for bottom 4-5 binary search levels: 69MB/8B = 8.6M entries, 2^23 levels cover 8M positions → bottom 3 levels hit DRAM at ~100ns each, giving ~400ns per probe × 8.9M / 64 threads = 55ms. Fix: two-level structured search. Build a compact AdshRange first-level index (one entry per unique adsh_code: {adsh_u, start_idx, end_idx}) from a single sequential scan of anti_data. With an estimated 100K–200K unique adsh codes, first-level = 1.2–2.4MB, well within the 44MB LLC. Level-1 binary search: log2(150K)=17 comparisons × 10ns L3 latency = 170ns. Level-2 linear scan within the sub-range: average ~50-80 entries = 400-640 bytes, fits in L1/L2 after the first probe hitting that adsh group (8.9M/150K ≈ 59 probes per adsh → thoroughly L1-resident). If adsh_code is absent from first-level, row is immediately an orphan with no second-level work. Per-probe cost drops from ~390ns (DRAM-heavy) to ~180ns (all L3/L2/L1): 2.2× speedup → main_scan ~25ms. Keep iter_1's parallel morsel dispatch (64 threads, CHUNK=8192), madvise(WILLNEED) on qualifying column ranges and entire anti array, per-thread GroupMap aggregation, and inline probe pattern — do NOT collect probe entries or use parallel sort (those caused the 333ms regression in iter_2).
